title: Kernel variant split-testing and safety contract
preview:
Summary

This document defines the safety contract, telemetry requirements, and a step-by-step split-testing procedure for rolling out new kernel variants in the serving runtime. It is intended for runtime engineers, release owners, and on-call SREs. The goal is to enable controlled experiments that validate latency...
text:
Summary

This document defines the safety contract, telemetry requirements, and a step-by-step split-testing procedure for rolling out new kernel variants in the serving runtime. It is intended for runtime engineers, release owners, and on-call SREs. The goal is to enable controlled experiments that validate latency, correctness, and resource-stability properties while providing deterministic rollback triggers.

Goals

- Allow safe A/B (split) testing of kernel variants (e.g., tiled-attention-v2, quantized-matmul-approx, async-dispatch) against production workloads.
- Define automated and human-reviewed rollback criteria that preserve customer SLOs and prevent resource exhaustion.
- Standardize telemetry and alerting required to make split decisions reproducible and auditable.
- Provide a compact, executable checklist for runbook-driven canary experiments.

Scope and non-goals

- Scop...

title: Representative Workload Suite (v1) — Perf Baseline & Regression Detection
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines the **Representative Workload Suite (v1)** used for:
- establishing the **Q1 2026 performance baselines** (latency/throughput) across regions and GPU SKUs

text:
## Purpose
This page defines the **Representative Workload Suite (v1)** used for:
- establishing the **Q1 2026 performance baselines** (latency/throughput) across regions and GPU SKUs
- detecting performance regressions in CI/perf-canary with production-like traffic shapes
- generating consistent workload cohorts for model catalog performance profiles

The suite is designed to mirror **production traffic shapes** without using or reconstructing sensitive prompt content (see ADR-0173). The workload definitions are stable, versioned, and fully reproducible via run-manifest capture (see ADR-0172).

## Non-goals
- This suite is not a customer-facing benchmark and is not optimized for headline numbers.
- v1 does not cover multimodal, audio, speculative decoding experiments, or fine-tuning endpoints.

---

## Suite structure at a glance
The suite is a set of workload generators + request-mi...

title: Runbook: Runtime overload mitigation (Hosted API)
preview:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to detect, triage, and mitigate **Serving Runtime overload** for Redwood Hosted API. It focuses on the runtime layer (scheduler, batching, KV cache, GPU utilization signals) and how to apply mitigations safely without causing retry storms or noisy-neighbor regressions.

This run...
text:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to detect, triage, and mitigate **Serving Runtime overload** for Redwood Hosted API. It focuses on the runtime layer (scheduler, batching, KV cache, GPU utilization signals) and how to apply mitigations safely without causing retry storms or noisy-neighbor regressions.

This runbook is used when:
- p95/p99 latency spikes and/or timeouts correlate with runtime saturation.
- Error rates increase due to runtime admission rejecting work (shed) or the gateway/routing breaker opening.
- One or more GPU pools show persistent queue growth or memory pressure.

## Scope
In scope:
- Runtime overload symptoms and saturation metrics
- Runtime mitigations (throttle/admission, batching caps, feature reduction toggles exposed to runtime)
- When/how to coordinate with Gateway and Smart Routing

Out of scope:
- Customer comms/status page updates (see Hosted circuit...

title: Deterministic Inference Mode: Reproducibility Spec & Runbook

text:
Summary:

This page defines the "Deterministic Inference Mode" (DIM): a configuration and operational contract that provides reproducible token output and execution traces across repeated inference runs for a pinned model variant and configuration. DIM is intended for debugging, eval reproducibility, and regression investigation where bit-for-bit repeatability (within documented tolerances) is required.

Goals:
- Provide a single switchable runtime mode (global or per-route) that reduces nondeterminism sources across scheduler, kernels, and model IO.
- Enumerate required kernel/runtime properties and configuration knobs to enable reproducible outputs for common models (decoder-only, encoder-decoder, multimodal pipelines).
- Provide an operator runbook for enabling DIM in canary and production, including telemetry, verification tests, and rollback criteria.

Non-goals:
- DIM is not a s...

title: Profiling mixed prefill/decode contention (mixed-workload tail latency)
preview:
## Purpose
This playbook documents how to reproduce and diagnose **mixed prefill/decode contention** that produces **user-visible p95 tail latency** regressions under mixed prompt-length distributions (e.g., short chat prompts interleaved with long-context prompts).

In steady-state homogeneous traffic, continuous b...
text:
## Purpose
This playbook documents how to reproduce and diagnose **mixed prefill/decode contention** that produces **user-visible p95 tail latency** regressions under mixed prompt-length distributions (e.g., short chat prompts interleaved with long-context prompts).

In steady-state homogeneous traffic, continuous batching tends to be stable. Under mixed workloads, we commonly see:
- **Head-of-line blocking**: long-prefill requests dominate GPU time and batch selection.
- **Scheduler instability**: rapid shifts between “prefill-heavy” and “decode-heavy” work.
- **Kernel selection thrash**: frequent switching of attention kernels/profiles as sequence lengths vary.
- **KV cache pressure**: eviction/compaction spikes that amplify decode latency.

This doc is written to be usable by Serving Runtime + Kernel teams and as a reference during canary triage.

---

## When to use this playbook
...

text:
## Purpose
This page is a **runtime-focused appendix** to the v1 incident taxonomy. It captures the most common *serving runtime* failure/regression patterns we see in production and how to:
1) Recognize them quickly via symptoms + metrics/logs
2) Apply a consistent **incident_type / subtype** classification
3) Use safe, repeatable **mitigations** to restore service

**Scope:** issues originating primarily in the serving runtime (GPU inference workers, batching/scheduler, KV cache, kernels, model load/serialization). This is not a root-cause framework; detailed RCA belongs in the postmortem.

**Primary owner:** Serving Runtime on-call. **Common consults:** SRE (incident process + comms), Infra/Fleet (capacity), Observability (metrics blind spots), Platform Routing (fallback behavior).

---

## Quick classification guidance (during a live incident)

title: Perf Canary Validation Plan (Staging Bake, Replay Tests, Noise Characterization, Acceptance Criteria)
preview:

text:
## Summary
This page defines the validation plan for the production performance canary (“perf-canary”) prior to enabling paging alerts in production. The plan covers:
- Staging bake (stability + correctness)
- Replay tests (controlled regressions + attribution)
- Noise characterization (variance, drift, seasonality, telemetry delays)
- Acceptance criteria and sign-off checklist for enabling paging

The intent is to ensure the canary is (1) sensitive to real regressions, (2) resilient to expected production variance, (3) low-noise, and (4) safe with respect to capacity overhead and data-handling.

## Scope
In scope:
- Synthetic prompt distribution (no customer data), representative of production prompt/response “shapes”
- Covered metrics: end-to-end latency (p50/p95/p99), runtime-only latency, prefill vs decode latency, tokens/sec, queueing time, batch size distribution, KV cache hit r...

title: Hardware Tuning Profiles Pack (H100/B200/L40S) — Project Overview
preview:

Redwood Inference will deliver a **hardware tuning profiles pack** for the serving runtime focused on **kernel + scheduler performance** across the current and near-future NVIDIA fleet: **H100, B200, L40S**.

The pack consists of **curated, versioned runtime configuration profiles** that are:

text:
## Summary
Redwood Inference will deliver a **hardware tuning profiles pack** for the serving runtime focused on **kernel + scheduler performance** across the current and near-future NVIDIA fleet: **H100, B200, L40S**.

The pack consists of **curated, versioned runtime configuration profiles** that are:
- **Safe-by-default** (guardrails, compatibility checks, sane fallbacks)
- **Measurable** (benchmarked against a workload matrix with tracked deltas)
- **Operationally rollout-friendly** (canaries, regression gates, rollback semantics)
- **Consistent across deployment modes** (Hosted, Dedicated, Private)

This page defines the project goals, non-goals, target SKUs, success metrics, ownership, and the milestone plan.

---

## Why we’re doing this
As our model catalog expands and fleet heterogeneity increases, performance issues increasingly come from **serving configuration mismatch** r...

title: Adaptive KV Coordinator and Eviction Contract
preview:
Overview

It is intended for runtime engineers, infra SREs, model-serving owners, and privat...
text:
Overview

This document specifies the runtime contract for the Adaptive KV Coordinator (AKC), the eviction policy interface, and the safety guarantees that downstream services (shard-manager, runtime-executor, request-router) rely on. It is intended for runtime engineers, infra SREs, model-serving owners, and private deployment integrators.

Goals

- Define a minimal, implementation-agnostic API for KV cache coordination across shards and variants.
- Provide deterministic eviction semantics under constrained memory pressure to avoid cross-variant nondeterminism.
- Specify telemetry, SLOs, and runtime knobs required for safe rollouts and private deployments.
- Outline a migration path and rollback plan for changing eviction strategies.

Scope and non-goals

- Scope: in-memory KV caches used for model KV caches (attention cache, prefix cache) and request-level prefix caches managed by r...

title: Hardware tuning profiles: schema + compatibility rules

The schema is designed to:

text:
## Summary
This document defines the **hardware tuning profile schema** and the **compatibility/guardrail rules** used by the serving runtime to apply safe, versioned tuning presets across GPU SKUs (H100, B200, L40S) and across deployment modes (Hosted, Dedicated, Private).

The schema is designed to:
- Keep profile changes **safe-by-default** (strict validation, bounded knobs, explicit intent).
- Make profile application **deterministic and debuggable** (explicit selection logic, metrics, logs).
- Prevent **silent drift** between runtime defaults and shipped profile packs (normalized defaults + compatibility constraints).

This is a design reference for implementation in `runtime-config` and the profile loader.

---

## Goals
1. Define a **profile pack format** with versioning and a stable schema.
2. Support **inheritance/overrides** to minimize duplication (common base → GPU family ...

title: Runtime concurrency isolation and telemetry handbook
preview:

text:
Overview
========
This handbook describes Redwood Inference's conventions and operational practices for runtime concurrency isolation, lane-based request shaping, and the minimum telemetry required to detect and diagnose cross-tenant or cross-model interference. It consolidates architecture rationale, configuration examples, diagnostics runbooks, and rollout guidance for changing isolation or sampling policies in production.

Goals
-----
- Ensure noisy tenants or long-context workloads do not violate p95/p99 latency SLOs for other tenants sharing GPUs.
- Provide a consistent taxonomy (lanes, credits, isolation zones) for engineers and SREs to reason about scheduling and fairness.
- Define telemetry, sampling, and alerting so incidents can be triaged without deep kernel-level tracing for every case.

Scope
-----
This doc covers: request lanes, concurrency caps, credit accounting surfac...

Summary:
This document defines an econometric model for estimating how changes in runtime decisions (kernel selection, batching, quantization, routing) map to observed latency and per-token cost, and provides a reproducible simulation runbook for validating sensitivity hypotheses before production rollouts. The goal is to provide a single reference for analysts and runtime engineers to: quantify marginal cost/latency effects, prioritize optimization work, and validate safe rollout envelopes for changes that affect operator economics or SLOs.

Contents:
1) Introduction and intent
2) Model specification and assumptions
3) Required telemetry and inputs
4) Simulation lab setup and dataset construction
5) Runbook: experiment steps (numbered)
6) Analysis and decision criteria
7) Example configs and sample output
8) Ownership and escalation

title: Perf Regression Canary (Prod) — Design: Runtime + Kernel/Scheduler Attribution
preview:
## 1. Summary
We will deploy a lightweight, always-on **performance canary** in production to detect and alert on **per-model** serving regressions before they broadly impact customers. The canary continuously runs a **synthetic, privacy-safe workload** against a curated model list in each enabled region/tier and ev...
text:
## 1. Summary
We will deploy a lightweight, always-on **performance canary** in production to detect and alert on **per-model** serving regressions before they broadly impact customers. The canary continuously runs a **synthetic, privacy-safe workload** against a curated model list in each enabled region/tier and evaluates results against **versioned baselines**.

Primary regression signals:
- **p95 end-to-end latency** and **p95 runtime-only latency**
- **tokens/sec** (throughput)

Secondary signals used for attribution and fast triage:
- Prefill vs decode latency
- Scheduler **queueing time** and batch size distribution
- KV cache hit rate (where applicable)
- Runtime build id, kernel selection id, scheduler config id

This document covers the architecture, workload model, metrics, baseline strategy, regression evaluation, attribution, and rollout plan.

## 2. Problem statement
Runt...

text:
## Purpose
This page defines the **kernel auto-selection rules** used for the **Q1 2026 performance baseline runs**. It is the canonical reference for:
- Which attention/GEMM kernels are expected for each **model family**
- Which fallbacks are acceptable (and when they are considered “unexpected”)
- Known performance cliffs and required **driver/CUDA/Triton** constraints for reproducibility

The goal is to make baseline results **attributable**. If a perf delta is observed, the first question should be: *did kernel selection change?*

---

## Scope
**In scope**
- Attention kernel selection (prefill + decode)
- KV-cache layout implications (paged vs contiguous) as they affect kernel eligibility
- Common model families: **Llama-family**, **Qwen-family**, **Mistral-family** variants used in baseline sweeps
- Hardware: **H100 (SXM/PCIe)**, **A100 80GB**, **L40S**

**Out of scope**
- Detai...

text:
## Purpose
This playbook describes how to reproduce a perf-suite result and capture the artifacts needed to attribute changes (runtime, scheduler, kernels, environment). It is intended for:
- Investigating perf-canary regressions (PR-level, nightly, or release-branch).
- Explaining deltas between baseline runs (Q1 2026 “golden” anchor) and candidate changes.
- Producing consistent traces/logs that can be shared across Runtime, Kernels, and SRE.

This page focuses on *how to profile* (traces, kernel timelines, scheduler logs) rather than *what workloads to run*. For workload definitions and required metrics, see the baseline plan and representative suite pages.

---

## What “good” looks like (minimum artifact set)
For any investigated run, attach or link the following artifacts (even if you also have dashboards):
1) Run manifest JSON (per ADR-0172) with *all* environment capture enabl...

title: Scheduler Compatibility Dependency Graph and Safety Orchestrator
preview:
Summary

This document defines the Scheduler Compatibility Dependency Graph (SCDG) and describes the Safety Orchestrator that enforces compatibility, graceful degradation, and safe rollouts between kernel variants, scheduler plugins, and runtime subsystems. It combines a static dependency model with dynamic runtime ...
text:
Summary

This document defines the Scheduler Compatibility Dependency Graph (SCDG) and describes the Safety Orchestrator that enforces compatibility, graceful degradation, and safe rollouts between kernel variants, scheduler plugins, and runtime subsystems. It combines a static dependency model with dynamic runtime checks and an automation layer that performs remediation and controlled fallback.

Why this exists

- Kernels and scheduler plugins are evolving rapidly (new quantization paths, sparse-attention kernels, fractional residency modes). We need a machine-readable compatibility model to avoid accidental unsafe pairings.
- Incidents historically involve combinations of kernel variants + admission-controller policies that produce deadlocks, soft OOMs, or priority inversion. The SCDG lets automation surface risky pairings before they reach production.

Scope and assumptions

- Spac...

title: Occupancy and Concurrency Audit Playbook for Serving Runtime
preview:
Overview:

This playbook describes a repeatable audit process to assess and remediate GPU occupancy and request concurrency issues in the Redwood serving runtime. It is intended for on-call engineers, performance engineers, and SREs responsible for multi-tenant inference pools and kernel scheduler tuning. The goal i...
text:
Overview:

This playbook describes a repeatable audit process to assess and remediate GPU occupancy and request concurrency issues in the Redwood serving runtime. It is intended for on-call engineers, performance engineers, and SREs responsible for multi-tenant inference pools and kernel scheduler tuning. The goal is to detect pathological occupancy/concurrency patterns early, quantify their impact on latency/cost, and prescribe targeted mitigations.

Why this matters:

- Low GPU occupancy (SM utilization) or poor warp efficiency increases per-token cost and can drastically raise tail latency for short requests.
- Excessive concurrency without appropriate kernel crediting can cause kernel contention, eviction churn, and KV-cache thrash.
- Multi-tenant pools and dynamic batching increase the surface area for occupancy anomalies; automated audits help prevent regressions when we change ...

title: Scheduler Policy Baseline (Q1 2026 Perf Suite)
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines the **scheduler configuration baseline** used for the **Q1 2026 Perf Baseline & Representative Workload Suite** runs.

The baseline is the set of scheduler knobs (and guardrails) that are considered “default-correct” for benchmarking and regression detection across:

text:
## Purpose
This page defines the **scheduler configuration baseline** used for the **Q1 2026 Perf Baseline & Representative Workload Suite** runs.

The baseline is the set of scheduler knobs (and guardrails) that are considered “default-correct” for benchmarking and regression detection across:
- Regions: **us-east**, **eu-west**
- GPU SKUs: **H100 / A100 / L40S** (where applicable)
- Workloads: chat streaming, tool/function calling, long-context prefill, embeddings high-QPS

The intent is to:
1) Make perf results comparable over time (reproducible, attributable).
2) Represent production behavior (no benchmark-only shortcuts that hide real latency sources).
3) Keep the scheduler explainable under tail latency (p95/p99) investigations.

This baseline is referenced by the run manifest schema (see ADR-0172) and is expected to be applied verbatim in baseline sweeps unless explicitly noted...

title: Q1 2026 Perf Baseline Plan (Serving Runtime)
preview:

This page defines the **Q1 2026 “golden” performance baselines** for Redwood’s serving runtime and the plan to produce them in a reproducible, CI-backed way.

The baseline is used for:
- Runtime release signoff (release branches)
- Perf-canary regression detection (nightly + gated modes)

text:
## Purpose
This page defines the **Q1 2026 “golden” performance baselines** for Redwood’s serving runtime and the plan to produce them in a reproducible, CI-backed way.

The baseline is used for:
- Runtime release signoff (release branches)
- Perf-canary regression detection (nightly + gated modes)
- Model catalog performance profiles (internal first; optional downstream publication)

## Goals (Q1 2026)
1) Publish baseline **latency/throughput** numbers for top deployed models across priority hardware and regions.
2) Establish a **representative workload taxonomy** that mirrors production traffic shapes.
3) Ensure results are **reproducible and attributable** (strict env capture, fixed workload seeds, manifests, trace linkage).
4) Integrate into automated canaries and release gating with clear thresholds for **regression vs noise**.

## Non-goals
- New model onboarding beyond what’s r...

title: Experiment Governance and Release Gates Playbook
preview:
Overview

This playbook defines governance, metric gates, and operational procedures for running experiments that change the serving runtime surface: kernel selection, scheduling policies, KV cache heuristics, quantization rollouts, and runtime admission controllers. It consolidates requirements from runtime archite...
text:
Overview

This playbook defines governance, metric gates, and operational procedures for running experiments that change the serving runtime surface: kernel selection, scheduling policies, KV cache heuristics, quantization rollouts, and runtime admission controllers. It consolidates requirements from runtime architecture, kernel scheduling, profiling, benchmarking, and release runbooks into a single actionable checklist for engineers and release managers.

Purpose and audience

- Purpose: Ensure safe, measurable, and auditable runtime experiments with clear release gates and rollback criteria.
- Audience: Runtime engineers, SREs, release managers, experiment owners, and applied ML engineers validating model-serving changes.

Scope

Applies to any change that touches the serving runtime execution path including but not limited to:
1) New or updated kernel implementations or kernel hint...

title: L40S tuned hardware profile reference (memory + batching presets)
preview:
## Purpose
This page documents the **L40S tuned runtime profile** in the Hardware Tuning Profiles Pack. It focuses on:
- **Memory constraints and KV-cache behavior** on L40S (48GB class, PCIe)
- **Safe-by-default batching / queue / backpressure presets**
- Guidance for **cost-sensitive workloads** where L40S is used...
text:
## Purpose
This page documents the **L40S tuned runtime profile** in the Hardware Tuning Profiles Pack. It focuses on:
- **Memory constraints and KV-cache behavior** on L40S (48GB class, PCIe)
- **Safe-by-default batching / queue / backpressure presets**
- Guidance for **cost-sensitive workloads** where L40S is used to reduce $/token while maintaining predictable latency

This is a reference doc ("expected behavior"), not a contractual guarantee. For operational rollout steps and rollback criteria, use the rollout runbook.

---

## Target hardware and assumptions
**GPU SKU:** NVIDIA L40S (PCIe)

**Key constraints vs H100-class fleets**
- **Tighter memory headroom** for long-context + high concurrency workloads. OOM risk is dominated by KV cache growth and fragmentation.
- **Lower peak prefill throughput** than H100; aggressive batching can improve $/token, but must be bounded to avoid...

text:
Overview

Purpose: This page defines the operator handoff checklist and the shared observability contract used by runtime engineers, SREs, and oncall to run, tune and troubleshoot Redwood's serving runtime. The contract covers the set of metrics, traces, profiling artifacts, benchmark markers, release-note signals, and known-issue flags that must be produced and consumed during a feature rollout or operational incident.

Scope
- Applies to runtime components that live in the serving process: dispatcher, kernel dispatcher, KV cache, scheduler, admission controller, telemetry adapter.
- Intended consumers: oncall SRE, runtime owners, release engineers, customer-facing support.

High-level principles
1) Contract-first telemetry: every feature must document the telem signals it produces and the expected shapes before canary.
2) Lightweight by default: default collection is cheap; heavy pr...

title: Rollout Runbook: Hardware Tuning Profiles Pack (H100/B200/L40S)

h1. Purpose
This runbook describes the operational procedure for safely rolling out the *Hardware Tuning Profiles Pack* for the serving runtime across *Hosted, Dedicated, and Private* deployments. It covers:

* Staged rollout by pool/region
* Required dashboards and gates
* Alert thresholds and stop/rollback criteria
* Triage and incident response playbook for regressions

The profiles pack provides curated, versioned runtime configuration presets per GPU SKU (*H100, B200, L40S*) and a consistent override model across fleet defaults and per-deployment overrides.

h1. Scope and assumptions
h2. In scope
* Enabling a specific *profile pack version* as the fleet default (or per-pool override)
* Enabling/disabling the profiles feature at runtime
* Rolling forward/back profile versions

## Purpose
This runbook describes how to safely enable and ramp the mixed-workload scheduler prototype (short/long prompt-aware scheduling) in production pools.

Primary goal: reduce user-visible p95 tail latency (TTFT and end-to-end) under mixed prompt-length distributions without materially regressing throughput or fairness.

This runbook covers:
- Preconditions and required sign-offs
- Feature flag enablement and safe parameter ramps
- Monitoring checklist and known-bad signals
- Rollback procedure
- Owner rotation and escalation

---

## Scope
Applies to runtime version(s) that include the prototype scheduler (currently: runtime-1.18+ candidate builds where the feature flag is present).

In-scope deployment targets:
- Hosted API: a single canary region/pool first (typically us-east)
- Dedicated: a small, opt-in subset of Dedicated GPU pools with mixed-workload symptoms

Out of sco...

text:
Summary

This document is a laboratory-grade procedure set for deterministic fault injection against the runtime scheduler and kernel handoff surface. It defines controlled experiments, safety gates, measurement artifacts, and a recovery playbook to be used by the Serving Runtime SRE/Platform teams when validating scheduler resilience, degradation policies and telemetry coverage.

Purpose

- Validate scheduler behaviour under targeted faults (node, kernel, eviction, preemption, telemetry loss).
- Ensure graceful degradation policies preserve SLOs within defined error budgets.
- Verify observability and automated recovery paths capture and mitigate incidents.

Scope

Applies to internal lab clusters (non-production), the runtime components listed below and the scheduler API surfaces used by runtime-operator and kernel-dispatcher. Not for production injection without executive approval ...

title: Mixed-workload scheduler prototype (short/long prompt-aware) — Design
preview:
## Summary
This document proposes a scheduler prototype to reduce user-visible p95 tail latency for **text generation** under **mixed prompt-length distributions** (short chat prompts interleaved with long-context prompts), without materially regressing throughput or fairness.

We introduce a **prompt-aware, class-b...
text:
## Summary
This document proposes a scheduler prototype to reduce user-visible p95 tail latency for **text generation** under **mixed prompt-length distributions** (short chat prompts interleaved with long-context prompts), without materially regressing throughput or fairness.

We introduce a **prompt-aware, class-based scheduling policy** that:
- Classifies requests into **SHORT** and **LONG** classes using prompt/context length and (optionally) a predicted prefill cost.
- Maintains **two queues** and applies a **biased dispatch policy** that favors SHORT while guaranteeing progress for LONG.
- Separates **prefill** and **decode** scheduling decisions to avoid long-prefill head-of-line (HoL) blocking.
- Adds guardrails: **aging**, **burst caps**, **max skew**, and **token budgets**.
- Ships behind a feature flag with safe defaults and a staged rollout plan.

Owner: Serving Runtime / ...

## Purpose
This page defines the runtime-level backpressure and saturation signals used by Hosted circuit breakers + load shedding. It documents:
- What signals the serving runtime emits (queues, throughput, KV cache, memory pressure, fragmentation proxies).
- Exact semantics and units.
- Export pipeline (metric types, sampling, aggregation, and cardinality constraints).
- How upstream systems should interpret the signals without creating feedback loops.

This is a reference for Runtime, SRE, Gateway/Control Plane, Smart Routing, and Observability.

---

**In scope**
- Signals emitted by the GPU-serving runtime at admission/scheduling boundaries.
- Metrics intended for near-real-time overload decisions (seconds).

- Per-request tracing details (handled by tracing pipeline).

text:
Summary
-------
This document defines a unified experiment scorecard and a minimal telemetry SLA for runtime experiments, kernel-level canaries, and benchmark-driven rollouts. It bridges architecture, kernel scheduling, profiling, benchmarking, release gating, and on-call runbooks so that engineers and SREs can run reproducible experiments with clear pass/fail criteria.

Scope
-----
- Applies to: runtime feature experiments, kernel variant canaries, quantization rollouts, and model-variant A/B tests run in hosted and dedicated deployments.
- Excludes: user-facing product A/B tests unrelated to inference runtime behavior.

Why this exists
----------------
We observed variability between bench microbenchmarks and live traffic (tail latency regressions, subtle token-cost changes, KV-cache warmup differences). That undermines rollouts. The scorecard + telemetry SLA ensures experiments hav...

The goal of the RCG is to keep customer-visible SLOs within targets while bounding per-token and per-request spend ac...
text:
Overview
========
This document defines the Fidelity Budgeting model and the Runtime Cost Governor (RCG) — a declarative control plane that enforces per-tenant/route fidelity budgets at serving time. The goal of the RCG is to keep customer-visible SLOs within targets while bounding per-token and per-request spend across mixed workloads.

Intended audience
- Runtime and scheduler engineers
- Product owners for Dedicated/Private customers
- SRE and capacity-planning teams
- Benchmark and profiling engineers

Why this exists
- Inference cost is variable by model variant, sequence length, batching shape and kernel choices.
- We need a single, auditable mechanism to trade fidelity for cost under pressure (autoscale, thermal events, capacity constraints).
- RCG ties scheduling signals, kernel hints, and quantization/variant routing decisions to a budget and provides a deterministic downgrad...

Overview

Purpose: This document catalogs the set of small, reproducible probes we run against the serving runtime to measure execution fidelity, kernel-scheduling sensitivity, and workload-driven regression risk. The catalog is intended for SREs, runtime engineers, and experimenters who need a concise set of checks to detect performance, correctness, or quality drift after kernel changes, model swaps, or deployment topology updates.

Scope: probes exercise kernel-dispatcher, kvcache behavior, sequence-length scaling, quantization alternatives, and cross-tenant memory policies. Each probe entry includes: objective, trigger conditions, exact workload or CLI invocation, expected telemetry signatures, SLO thresholds, and immediate remediation runbook.

text:
## Purpose
This runbook helps on-call responders triage GPU saturation incidents using **Incident Bot v2** saturation cards and standard Redwood dashboards. It maps bot-reported signals (GPU utilization, queue depth, KV cache pressure, scheduler/backpressure indicators) to likely causes and safe mitigations.

**Use this when:**
- p95/p99 latency spikes, time-to-first-token (TTFT) regresses, or 5xx/streaming disconnects correlate with **high utilization or queueing**.
- A region/pool is “hot” (capacity constrained) and routing/fallback decisions are needed.

**Primary goal:** restore SLOs quickly while preserving safety (tenant isolation, rate limits, and bounded telemetry).

---

## Scope and assumptions
Covers:
- Hosted API (multi-tenant pools)
- Dedicated pools (single-tenant, reserved capacity)
- Private (VPC/on-prem) where metrics are available via the customer’s telemetry stack

## Purpose
This runbook describes the end-to-end procedure to cut a **Runtime LTS patch release** (example: `1.18.1-lts`) from an LTS branch (example: `runtime-1.18-lts`). It is designed for Release Engineering + Runtime + SRE to produce:
- A patch containing **approved backports only** (security, Sev1/Sev2 reliability, correctness fixes)
- **Reproducible, auditable** artifacts (checksums/signatures/provenance; SBOM where applicable)
- **Perf-validated** binaries (gated benchmark suite + waiver workflow)
- Enterprise-grade **slower-change release notes**
- Coordinated rollouts across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private packaging

## Scope
Applies to:
- LTS patch releases for the serving runtime (kernel + scheduling + runtime core)
- Standard monthly cadence patches and out-of-band security hotfixes

Not covered:
- Cutting a new LTS branch (see ADR-027)
- Feature releases on `main`

- **Important enough to track across patches** (even if already mitigated)

If an issue is fixed in mainline but **not eligible** for backport to LTS, it should still be tracked here with a documented workaround and rationale.

title: Quantization Profile Perf Benchmark Suite (v1)
preview:

The suite is designed to answer four questions per profile/model/hardware combination:
- **Cost/token impact:** Does this profile reduce effe...
text:
## Summary
This document defines the standard performance benchmark suite used to evaluate Quantization Profile Library candidates and published profiles across key GPU SKUs.

The suite is designed to answer four questions per profile/model/hardware combination:
- **Cost/token impact:** Does this profile reduce effective compute cost per generated token relative to baseline precision?
- **Throughput impact:** What is the steady-state tokens/sec and requests/sec under realistic concurrency?
- **VRAM impact:** Does the profile reduce, increase, or destabilize VRAM usage (weights + KV cache + workspace)?
- **Tail latency impact:** How does p95/p99 latency change across representative prompt/response shapes?

This suite is used by:
- Applied ML (profile authoring and offline validation)
- Serving Runtime (kernel selection, batching, KV cache behavior)
- SRE/Release Engineering (perf-canar...

## Design principles (what “slower-change” means)
1) **Every change is categorized and justified** (why it’s safe / why it matters).

title: Quantization profile selection in runtime (v1)
preview:
## Summary
This document describes how the Serving Runtime selects and applies an opt-in Quantization Profile (QP) for a request. The goals for v1 are:

- Deterministic selection and application (same inputs => same profile decision).
- Safety by default (feature off => no behavior change; incompatible/failed apply ...
text:
## Summary
This document describes how the Serving Runtime selects and applies an opt-in Quantization Profile (QP) for a request. The goals for v1 are:

- Deterministic selection and application (same inputs => same profile decision).
- Safety by default (feature off => no behavior change; incompatible/failed apply => safe fallback).
- Low overhead at high QPS (aggressive caching; bounded fetch retries; no thundering herd).
- Clear observability (request-level attribution and aggregate metrics for applied vs fallback).

This page focuses on the runtime path only. Compatibility policy evaluation and route config resolution occur in the control plane/policy engine; the runtime consumes the resolved decision.

## Terminology
- **Route**: customer-configured endpoint mapping to a model variant + settings.
- **Resolved quant decision**: the policy engine output attached to the request (or ...

The LTS channel exists to support Dedicated and Private customers who need a stable kernel + scheduling baseline for 6–12+ months with:

text:
## Summary
This document defines Redwood Inference’s Long Term Support (LTS) strategy for the serving runtime and the policy for backporting fixes onto LTS branches.

The LTS channel exists to support Dedicated and Private customers who need a stable kernel + scheduling baseline for 6–12+ months with:
- Predictable patch cadence
- Clear eligibility criteria for backports (security/Sev1/Sev2)
- Strong correctness + performance gates
- Reproducible, auditable release artifacts

This policy is intentionally conservative: LTS prioritizes stability and operational predictability over feature parity with main.

---

## Goals
- Provide an LTS branch line (e.g., `runtime-1.18-lts`) with a defined support window.
- Establish a predictable patch cadence (monthly) plus an out-of-band (OOB) process for urgent security fixes.
- Define what is eligible for backport, how risk is assessed, required t...

title: TP stall triage playbook (tensor-parallel hangs/timeouts)
preview:
## Purpose
This playbook covers step-by-step triage for **tensor-parallel (TP) stalls** (a.k.a. hangs/timeouts) in the serving runtime. It is written for on-call SRE + runtime engineers to quickly answer:
- Is the stall **compute**, **NCCL/collectives**, or **scheduler/starvation**?
- Which replicas/ranks are stuck,...
text:
## Purpose
This playbook covers step-by-step triage for **tensor-parallel (TP) stalls** (a.k.a. hangs/timeouts) in the serving runtime. It is written for on-call SRE + runtime engineers to quickly answer:
- Is the stall **compute**, **NCCL/collectives**, or **scheduler/starvation**?
- Which replicas/ranks are stuck, and is it isolated or systemic?
- What data should we capture (metrics/logs/traces/NCCL debug) without making the incident worse?

This is **triage + profiling**. For mitigation actions (drain/replace, concurrency caps, feature flags), use the runbook: *Runtime TP hang mitigation*.

---

## Definitions (what we mean by “TP stall”)
A TP stall is a condition where a multi-GPU replica stops making forward progress for a request or decode loop. Typical external symptoms:
- Streaming responses stop mid-stream (client sees partial tokens then timeout).
- Request p99 and timeout ...

title: Known issue: Tensor-parallel hangs under sustained load
preview:
## Summary
Tensor-parallel (TP) replicas can intermittently **hang (no forward progress)** under sustained, mixed workloads (prefill + decode, long-context, bursty concurrency). This typically presents as **streaming responses stalling** and eventually **request timeouts**. In the worst case, one or more GPUs appear...
text:
## Summary
Tensor-parallel (TP) replicas can intermittently **hang (no forward progress)** under sustained, mixed workloads (prefill + decode, long-context, bursty concurrency). This typically presents as **streaming responses stalling** and eventually **request timeouts**. In the worst case, one or more GPUs appear “wedged” until the replica is drained/restarted.

This page documents:
- Observable symptoms and common signatures
- Affected configurations (models, TP sizes, hardware)
- How to detect and confirm a TP stall
- Recommended mitigations (operational + config)
- Fix status by runtime version and feature flags

> If you are actively in an incident: jump to **Immediate mitigations** and then follow **Triage**.

---

## Impact / why we care
- **Customer impact:** Streaming completions stall; end-users see long hangs then timeouts. Enterprise Dedicated accounts are most affected ...
links: Linear: ENG-4821 Multi-GPU (TP) stability fixes initiative

title: TP Stability Fixes (Multi-GPU) — Design: Hang Taxonomy, Root Causes, and Runtime Hardening Plan
preview:
## Summary
Tensor-parallel (TP) replicas (2–8 GPUs per replica) are now the dominant mode for our largest Dedicated clusters and several high-throughput Hosted workloads. Under sustained mixed load (prefill + decode, long context, bursty concurrency, client cancels), we have observed failure modes that manifest as:
...
text:
## Summary
Tensor-parallel (TP) replicas (2–8 GPUs per replica) are now the dominant mode for our largest Dedicated clusters and several high-throughput Hosted workloads. Under sustained mixed load (prefill + decode, long context, bursty concurrency, client cancels), we have observed failure modes that manifest as:
- “Silent stalls” (no progress, GPUs appear busy or idle, requests hang)
- Downstream request timeouts (streaming or non-streaming)
- Autoscaling thrash due to inflated p99/p999
- Occasional Sev1 incidents requiring draining/replacing replicas

This document proposes a targeted hardening plan focused on **correctness + stability** (not a TP architecture rewrite), with an emphasis on:
1) A clear **taxonomy** of stall/hang signatures
2) Concrete **root-cause hypotheses** mapped to instrumentation and fixes
3) Scheduler and comms-boundary changes that reduce the probability of...

Summary:
This playbook describes recommended approaches for reducing cold-start latency and request output entropy during model/worker startup in Redwood’s serving runtime. It consolidates operational patterns, automated warmup recipes, example configs, and runbook steps for diagnosing and recovering from cold-start–related SLO breaches. The guidance applies to hosted, dedicated, and private deployments.

Scope:
- Applies to long-lived model workers and ephemeral worker pools (autoscaled GPUs).
- Focus on first-token latency, tail latency during burst scaling, and early-session output quality variability caused by incomplete KV cache or lazy kernel initialization.
- Excludes model training workflows and offline batch workloads.

Background and problem statement:
Cold starts in the runtime manifest in three measurable ways:
1) high first-byte/first-token latency before kernels and memo...

title: TP Stress Suite Spec (Multi-GPU Tensor Parallel Reliability + Performance)
preview:
## Summary
This document specifies the **TP Stress Suite**: a set of repeatable workloads and pass/fail criteria used to detect **multi-GPU tensor-parallel (TP)** regressions in Redwood’s serving runtime. The suite targets known failure modes under sustained load (mixed prefill/decode, long context, bursty concurren...
text:
## Summary
This document specifies the **TP Stress Suite**: a set of repeatable workloads and pass/fail criteria used to detect **multi-GPU tensor-parallel (TP)** regressions in Redwood’s serving runtime. The suite targets known failure modes under sustained load (mixed prefill/decode, long context, bursty concurrency, cancellation, memory/KV pressure) that have historically produced **request hangs, GPU stalls, and downstream timeouts**.

The suite runs in two modes:
1) **CI smoke + gating** (fast, minimal hardware): catches obvious deadlocks and correctness-breaking changes.
2) **Perf lab + perf-canary** (representative GPUs, longer burn): catches probabilistic stalls, p99 latency inflation, and throughput regressions.

## Goals
- Detect and prevent re-introduction of TP hangs/timeouts (primary).
- Provide early, actionable signals that localize stalls to **compute vs NCCL/collectiv...

title: Runbook: Runtime TP hang mitigation (multi-GPU replicas)
preview:
## Purpose
This runbook covers immediate, on-call mitigation for **tensor-parallel (TP) hangs/stalls** in multi-GPU replicas (Dedicated and high-throughput Hosted). It focuses on keeping customer traffic healthy while preserving enough signal for follow-up debugging.

**Primary objectives (in order):**
1) Stop user-...
text:
## Purpose
This runbook covers immediate, on-call mitigation for **tensor-parallel (TP) hangs/stalls** in multi-GPU replicas (Dedicated and high-throughput Hosted). It focuses on keeping customer traffic healthy while preserving enough signal for follow-up debugging.

**Primary objectives (in order):**
1) Stop user-visible timeouts / p99 blowups.
2) Prevent a wedged TP replica from accumulating more load.
3) Capture minimal, high-signal diagnostics.
4) Escalate with a complete package to Runtime (Kernel/Scheduling) and SRE.

---

## Definitions
- **TP hang / stall:** A replica continues to accept traffic but makes no forward progress on one or more TP ranks (often visible as a heartbeat gap, in-flight collectives stuck, or streaming decode never producing tokens).
- **Replica drain:** Stop routing new requests to a replica, allow in-flight requests to complete (or fail fast), then res...

title: Automatic fallback policy behavior (Hosted API)
preview:
# Summary
This page documents the behavior of **automatic fallback** in the Redwood serving runtime for the **Hosted API**. This policy is used during normal operations and is often enabled during model rollouts to reduce customer-visible errors during capacity or instability events.

# What fallback does
When fallb...
text:
# Summary
This page documents the behavior of **automatic fallback** in the Redwood serving runtime for the **Hosted API**. This policy is used during normal operations and is often enabled during model rollouts to reduce customer-visible errors during capacity or instability events.

# What fallback does
When fallback is enabled on a route, the router may send requests to a **fallback model** instead of the primary model based on defined triggers. The request remains a standard Hosted API request; the router modifies the upstream target only.

# Fallback triggers
Fallback can activate for these reasons (not exhaustive):
- **Capacity pressure**: primary pool is saturated (queue time above threshold).
- **Elevated error rate**: primary 5xx above threshold over a short window.
- **Cold start or rollout warmup**: primary replicas not ready or still ramping.
- **Regional impairment**: a s...

title: Caching & Batching Cost Signals (Serving Runtime Profiling Playbook)
preview:
## Purpose
This playbook documents the serving-runtime signals we rely on to *explain* changes in unit cost (blended $/1M output tokens). It focuses on the three most common runtime drivers:

1) **Batching utilization** (how much GPU time is spent doing useful work vs waiting/underfilled)
2) **KV/prefix cache effect...
text:
## Purpose
This playbook documents the serving-runtime signals we rely on to *explain* changes in unit cost (blended $/1M output tokens). It focuses on the three most common runtime drivers:

1) **Batching utilization** (how much GPU time is spent doing useful work vs waiting/underfilled)
2) **KV/prefix cache effectiveness** (how much compute we avoid by reusing previous work)
3) **Prefill vs decode mix** (how request shape and context length shift GPU cost per output token)

This page is referenced by the Unit Economics dashboard and the on-call discrepancy playbooks. It is intended for Serving Runtime, Telemetry/Analytics, and SRE.

---

## How runtime signals map to blended $/1M output tokens
### The mental model
At a high level:

**blended $/1M output tokens** ≈ (GPU $ + overhead allocations) / (output tokens) * 1,000,000

When the metric moves, one (or more) of these must be true...
